title: Entanglement transitions in translation-invariant tensor networks
authors: Yi-Cheng Wang, Samuel J. Garratt, Ehud Altman
abstract: We study the complexity of approximately contracting translation-invariant tensor networks. The computational cost of row-by-row tensor network contraction, which defines a discrete time evolution governed by a fixed transfer matrix, is associated with the entanglement of the state of a row. By analyzing a family of tensor networks whose transfer matrices interpolate between chaotic Floquet and strongly non-unitary limits, we uncover a transition between volume- and area-law entanglement in states evolved under the transfer matrix. We show that deep in the volume-law phase the spectrum of the transfer matrix in the complex plane consists of a dense ring with a sharp outer edge, reminiscent of behavior identified for non-unitary random matrices. At late times an evolving row state therefore has significant contributions from many eigenvectors with nearly degenerate eigenvalue magnitudes. In the area-law phase, there is instead a distinct leading eigenvalue. Our results establish connections between contraction complexity, spectral properties of the transfer matrix, and purification under non-unitary dynamics.
summary: The paper identifies a phase transition in the entanglement scaling of 2D translation-invariant tensor networks driven by non-unitarity. It connects the computational cost of network contraction to the spectral properties of the transfer matrix, specifically showing how a dense ring of eigenvalues in the volume-law phase gives way to a spectral gap in the area-law phase.
main_problem: Investigating the complexity of contracting translation-invariant 2D tensor networks and identifying the transition between volume-law and area-law entanglement in boundary states.
main_result: The authors discover a transition from a volume-law to an area-law entanglement phase driven by non-unitarity, characterized by a spectral transition in the transfer matrix from a dense ring of eigenvalues to a distinct leading eigenvalue.
method: Numerical simulations of row-state evolution, Matrix Product State (MPS) approximations, transfer matrix spectral analysis, and comparison with Random Matrix Theory (RMT) and mean-field theory.
model_or_system: A 2D cluster state on a square lattice with periodic boundary conditions, modeled as a non-unitary version of the kicked Ising model where the transfer matrix acts as a 1D time evolution operator.
key_observables: Half-chain entanglement entropy, antipodal mutual information, purification time, radial eigenvalue density, and the spectral gap of the transfer matrix.
important_parameters: Non-unitary field strength (h_I), real field (h_R), and interaction strength (J).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on translation-invariant networks, though results are tested for robustness against spatial disorder.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the tensor network contraction schematic; Figure 2 displays the entanglement entropy and mutual information transitions; Figure 3 illustrates the transfer matrix spectral gap and eigenvalue density scaling; Figure 4 discusses computational tractability via bond dimension scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the tensor network contraction problem, describes the non-unitary kicked Ising model, presents numerical results for entanglement and purification transitions, analyzes the spectral properties of the transfer matrix, tests the universality/robustness of the results, and concludes with implications for computational complexity.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems as it links the complexity of classical tensor network contraction to fundamental physical phenomena like entanglement transitions and non-unitary purification dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the scalar amplitude (θ, ϕ|Ψ). The physical indices of the projected entangled pair state |Ψ) are contracted with a product state (θ, ϕ| (blue), reducing the system to an effective 2D tensor network with only virtual bonds. (b) Interpretation of the contracted tensor network as a time evolution. The row-to-row transfer matrix T (blue shading) propagates the initial boundary state |ψi⟩(red shading) along the vertical temporal direction t, generating the effective boundary state |ψ(t)⟩.

FIG. 2. Entanglement and purification transitions in the non-unitary kicked Ising model, driven by increasing hI with hR = π/6. (a) Half-chain entanglement entropy SL/2 at time t = 4L as a function of non-unitary field strength hI for system sizes L = 12 to 24. (b) Antipodal mutual information IAB, with region sizes |A| = |B| = L/6 and separation rA,B = L/2 (inset), at time t = 4L as a function of hI for system sizes L = 12, 18, 24. The IAB of the leading eigenstates, indicated by dashed lines, correspond to the infinite time limit. In (a) and (b) we average the initial row state over random translation-invariant product states. (c) Entanglement entropy of a single reference qubit that is...

FIG. 3. Spectral transition in the transfer matrix T. (a) The spectral gap ∆ρ ≡ρ0 −ρ1 as a function of non-unitary field strength hI for even system sizes L = 12, 14, . . . , 24 and real field hR = π/6. A constant gap appears at hI >∼0.2, while there are fluctuations and frequent crossings of leading eigenvalues at hI <∼0.2 [30]. Inset: Gap between logarithms of the magnitudes of the leading eigenvalues at small hI. For each system size L, the multiple kinks in ∆ρ arise from crossings of magnitudes of the largest eigenvalues, which generically have different phases ϕα. (b) Collapse of radial eigenvalue density n(ρ) for L = 16, 18, 20 and several non-unitary fields hI ∈[0.005, 0.1]...

FIG. 4. Computational tractability transition in finite-size and infinite systems. (a) Half-chain Rényi-0.5 entropy S0.5 of the long-time-evolved state (t = 4L) at several system sizes L as a function of the non-unitary field hI. (b) Half-chain von Neumann entropy Sχ of the infinite chain at time t = 100 for several bond dimensions χ as a function of hI. Inset: At hI = 0.06, 0.08, 0.10 (increasing opacity) in the volume-law phase, Sχ scales linearly in ln χ.
